title: Actions to Address Fire Risk in Landscape Maintenance District (LMD) Areas
resolved_text:
Memorandum
DATE: March 27, 2026
TO: Mayor and City Council
THROUGH: Jackie Bryant, City Manager
FROM: David Cochran, Fire Chief
  Talina Sky, Fire Inspector II
  Nathan Ullyot, Director
DEPT: Reno Fire Department
  Parks & Recreation
SUBJECT: Actions to Address Fire Risk in Landscape Maintenance District (LMD) Areas

In response to recent articles and inquiries regarding fire risk associated with vegetation conditions in Landscape Maintenance District (LMD) areas, the Reno Fire Department (RFD) coordinated with the Parks and Recreation Department to review current maintenance practices and identify actions to reduce wildfire risk adjacent to residential areas.

Background

Landscape Maintenance Districts (LMDs) are maintained by the Parks Maintenance Division through a contracted landscaping company, with oversight provided by Parks Maintenance Division staff. Routine maintenance includes vegetation management, weed removal, debris cleanup, and clearing along fence lines and other landscaped areas.

Wildfire defensible space guidelines recommend maintaining approximately five feet of clearance from homes, outbuildings, and fences to reduce the potential for fire spread. In areas where larger trees cannot reasonably be removed, pruning is recommended to prevent limbs from overhanging fences or directing fire toward adjacent properties.

Findings and Actions

• Vegetation Maintenance Oversight – LMD areas are maintained by a contracted landscaping company operating under the direction and supervision of Parks Maintenance staff. Maintenance activities include weed removal, debris cleanup, and vegetation clearing along fence lines and landscaped areas. Identified issues or deficiencies are addressed as soon as possible.

• Defensible Space Implementation – A five-foot vegetation clearance along fence lines will be established within LMD areas in accordance with defensible space guidelines. Defensible space implementation will prioritize the removal of combustible vegetation while recognizing that certain existing, maintained vegetation may be retained where removal is not practical or would conflict with established maintenance objectives. In cases where large diameter trees are to remain, pruning will be conducted to reduce overhanging limbs and minimize potential fire pathways. Application of these standards may vary based on site conditions and the relative severity of wildfire risk in specific areas.

• Contract Review and Updates – The current LMD maintenance contract expires September 30 and the LMD maintenance contract will be rebid this summer. RFD will assist the Parks and Recreation Department in reviewing contract specifications to ensure vegetation maintenance requirements align with defensible space and wildfire risk reduction practices.

• Training and Coordination – RFD will provide defensible space training and wildfire risk reduction guidance to the contracted landscaping company to support consistent maintenance practices.

• Site Verification – RFD will work with the Parks and Recreation Department to conduct site visits in LMD areas to verify that defensible space guidelines and vegetation management practices are being implemented appropriately.

These coordinated efforts between RFD and the Parks and Recreation Department are intended to strengthen vegetation management practices, reduce wildfire risk near residential areas, and ensure defensible space principles are consistently applied within City-maintained landscape areas.

fields.authors: David Cochran, Fire Chief; Talina Sky, Fire Inspector II; Nathan Ullyot, Director
fields.tldr: The Reno Fire Department and Parks & Recreation Department are coordinating to reduce wildfire risk in Landscape Maintenance District (LMD) areas by improving vegetation management practices. A five-foot defensible space clearance along fence lines will be established, and large trees will be pruned to reduce fire pathways. The current LMD maintenance contract expires September 30 and will be rebid this summer, with RFD input to align specs with defensible space standards. RFD will also provide training to the contracted landscaping company and conduct site verification visits.
fields.key_stats: 5-foot vegetation clearance standard along fence lines in LMD areas; LMD maintenance contract expires September 30, 2026.
fields.key_decisions: Establish five-foot defensible space clearance along LMD fence lines; rebid LMD maintenance contract this summer with RFD-reviewed specifications; provide defensible space training to contracted landscaping company; conduct joint RFD/Parks site verification visits.
fields.action_items: 1) Establish 5-ft vegetation clearance along LMD fence lines. 2) Prune large-diameter trees to reduce overhanging limbs. 3) RFD to assist Parks & Recreation in reviewing and updating LMD contract specifications before rebid this summer. 4) RFD to provide defensible space training to contracted landscaping company. 5) RFD and Parks & Recreation to conduct joint site visits to verify implementation.

projects: Defensible Space Implementation in LMD Areas
